snippet.title: Nuxt's Lead Daniel Roe on the Truth Behind "Just Use AI to Write It"
snippet.description:
🎙️ Nuxt lead Daniel Roe explains why reaching for AI to write your messages ends up obscuring the one thing that matters most: your own voice.

He argues that real collaboration in open source is about the person behind the words, not just the facts an LLM can neatly summarize.

Along the way he unpacks Nuxt, the Nitro server behind sub-millisecond cold starts, how the edge actually works, and what makes a great developer in an age of hype.

🎙️ Daniel Roe leads the Nuxt core team and works on Nuxt full-time at Vercel, shaping tools that developers rely on worldwide. A former SaaS CTO and creative agency founder, he consults globally on JAMstack, serverless, and software architecture, and is based in Scotland with his family and cat.
🤝 Connect with Daniel 
🔗 https://github.com/danielroe
🔗 https://linkedin.com/in/daniel-roe
🔗 https://bsky.app/profile/danielroe.dev
🧑‍💻  Explore his work:
🔗 https://roe.dev/

💫 Our host, Chris Griffing, is a Twitch streamer and YouTuber who loves building things. Catch him live and join his growing developer community at https://twitch.tv/cmgriffing 🔗

🕐 Timestamps:
00:00 - Meet Daniel Roe — born in Alabama, leading Nuxt from Scotland
01:20 - From asking questions on Discord to running the whole project
03:29 - What studying law secretly taught him about writing code
05:14 - How they rebuilt Nuxt and crushed a 300ms cold start (down to under 1ms)
10:55 - The rarest move in open source: giving your own project away
11:50 - So what actually is "the edge"?
16:00 - Why your chair matters as much as your dev tools
19:34 - The design choice that hands the power back to you
21:10 - Vercel bought Nuxt Labs — so who owns Nuxt now?
22:39 - React Summit, and the talk he rewrote in the shower
25:32 - When strangers tear into your work online
28:03 - You don't "break into" open source — do this instead
30:16 - The open-source problem money can't fix
34:24 - Stop using AI to talk to me
37:52 - Wrapping up

This podcast was recorded at React Summit US 2025. Want to hear industry leaders like Luca IRL? Join our next events!

snippet.title: Next.js Is Changing Navigation Again - To Fix The Click Delay (16.3) | Aurora Scharff
snippet.description:
Why do Next.js apps still feel slow after a click - even with server rendering? 🤔 Aurora Scharff (Next.js team, Vercel) breaks down how RSCs bring back component composability, and how Next.js 16.3 closes the ~100ms gap that used to make SPAs feel faster than servers ⚡

What's inside:
📌 How data fetching evolved from useEffect - and why RSCs finally fixed the composability problem all of those created
📌 Real benchmark numbers: 5.1s (client-side fetch) vs 2.15s (getServerSideProps) vs 1.28s (RSC + Suspense)
📌 Live demo: Cache Components (use cache, cacheTag, cacheLife), Suspense boundaries, and the new 16.3 instant navigation via app shells + partial prefetching

🎙 Aurora Scharff is a DX Engineer on the Next.js team at Vercel. She's also the React Certification Lead at certificates.dev, where she helps set the bar for how the industry validates React expertise. 
💫 Connect with Aurora:
🔗 https://twitter.com/aurorascharff
🔗 https://github.com/aurorascharff
🔗 https://linkedin.com/in/aurora-scharff-a86b88188
💫 Read Aurora's blog:
🔗 https://aurorascharff.no/

📝 Talk: What RSCs Can Do in Next.js Today | Aurora Scharff

This talk was recorded at React Summit Amsterdam 2026: 
🔗 https://reactsummit.com/?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=AuroraScharff

📌 Follow the link to watch the full version of all the conference talks, QnA’s with speakers and hands-on workshop recordings:  
🔗 https://gitnation.com/events/react-summit-2026?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=AuroraScharff

🕐 Timestamps:
00:00 - Intro
00:41 - Why React (And Agents) Love Composability
02:21 - Where Data Fetching Breaks Everything
05:14 - How RSCs Bring Composability Back
07:11 - The Wishlist: What We Actually Want From Apps
07:55 - The Benchmark: 5.1s vs 2.15s vs 1.28s
09:06 - What's Still Broken (And The 100ms Rule)
11:48 - Next.js 16.3: Making Clicks Feel Instant
13:26 - Building Next.js For The Age Of AI Agents
14:09 - Live Demo: Building An Instant Music App
28:51 - Recap & Try The Demo Yourself

snippet.title: AI wrote code, but forgot about performance - fix it with 1 prompt | Angel Pichardo, ImageKit.io
snippet.description:
AI built your app. It didn't optimize your images. Here's how 1 MCP prompt takes you from 68MB to 2MB – automatically ⚡

What's inside:
✅ How Chrome DevTools MCP diagnoses real performance issues in your live app
✅ How ImageKit MCP reads its own docs and generates the fix without you leaving the editor
✅ Why image optimization is now a prompt, not a task

🎙 Angel Pichardo is a Senior Developer Advocate at ImageKit.
10+ years in engineering, now building at the intersection of AI agents and developer tooling. His talks skip the theory: he starts with a broken app and fixes it live.
⚡️ Connect with Angel:
🔗 https://twitter.com/_AngelPichardo
🔗 https://github.com/gitpichardo
⚡️ Explore what ImageKit builds:
🔗 https://imagekit.io/

📝 Talk: I Did Everything Wrong So You Don't Have To | Angel Pichardo

This talk was recorded at React Summit Amsterdam 2026: 
🔗 https://reactsummit.com/?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=AngelPichardo

📌 Follow the link to watch the full version of all the conference talks, QnA’s with speakers and hands-on workshop recordings:

🕐 Timestamps:
00:00 - "Wait, why are my images so slow?" - the moment it all breaks
00:48 - The old way: docs, guesswork, manual fixes. Never again
01:13 - Live demo: 1 prompt diagnoses your entire app
03:44 - Performance is always an afterthought. Here's how to stop that
04:43 - One URL. 3.28MB → 47KB. Watch it happen.
05:31 - Do this before you ship. Every time.

snippet.title: Who reviews your agent's code? | Santosh Yadav, CodeRabbit
snippet.description:
🤖 AI writes code faster than you can review it. Here's how engineers are adapting – and what comes next 👈

What's inside:
❓ CLI, IDE, or agentic loop — which review style actually works in 2026
❓ Why the 18-year-old GitHub diff view is now your biggest bottleneck
❓ How to review AI-generated code without losing your mind (or your database)

🎙 Santosh Yadav is a Principal Developer Advocate at CodeRabbit - an AI code review platform trusted by thousands of engineering teams worldwide. He's a Google Developer Expert for Angular, GitHub Star, Nx Champion, and Microsoft MVP. Santosh actively contributes to Angular and its ecosystem, champions monorepo architecture, and has been helping developers ship better code for years.
💫 Connect with Santosh:

snippet.title: I Ship a Production App With No Backend – This Is the Architecture | Mikkel Malmberg, Tether
snippet.description:
🔥 What if every new user made your app stronger – not your bill bigger? We show how they shipped Keet, a production messenger, with zero centralized backend – using cryptographic keys, signed messages, and deterministic state sync instead.

👉 This is local-first, P2P architecture that actually runs in production.

What's inside:
✅ Why your server is just a reducer — and how to remove it from the equation completely
✅ How to replace auth, storage, and state sync with cryptography and a deterministic log
✅ The Peer SDK Tether is building so any developer can ship P2P apps without thinking about the crypto layer

🎙 Mikkel Malmberg is a programmer and indie app builder – founder of Brainbow, a tiny product studio building platforms for creators. He's been shipping macOS apps, tools, and side projects since 2007, and recently stumbled into P2P.
Connect with Mikkel:
🔗 x.com/mikker
Explore what he ships:
🔗 mikkelmalmberg.com
Learn more about Tether:
🔗 tether.io

📝 Talk: Speed, Quality, and AI: You Can't Have It All (Or Can You?) | Mikkel Malmberg

This talk was recorded at React Summit Amsterdam 2026: 
🔗 https://reactsummit.com/?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=MikkelMalmberg

📌 Follow the link to watch the full version of all the conference talks, QnA’s with speakers and hands-on workshop recordings:  
🔗 https://gitnation.com/events/react-summit-2026?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=MikkelMalmberg

🕐 Timestamps:
00:00 - The problem: more users = bigger bills
01:55 - What if users helped instead of cost you?
03:35 - P2P basics: replacing the server with cryptography
06:12 - Distributed state: Redux without a server
09:07 - Keet: this architecture in production
09:23 - Peer SDK: build P2P apps without the crypto layer

snippet.title: Claude Code, Codex, Gemini – parallel agents in one editor | Zed live demo | Gaauwe Rombouts
snippet.description:
Run Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini in parallel – without leaving your editor. 💫 Gaauwe Rombouts from Zed shows how agentic workflows actually work in practice, live on stage at React Summit ⚡

What's inside:
✅ How to run multiple AI agents simultaneously in isolated work trees — no context switching, no friction
✅ Zed's open agent architecture: use any agent (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini) through one unified interface
✅ Live demo: real mistakes, real fixes, real parallel builds — what actually happens when you let agents loose

🎙 Gaauwe Rombouts is a Frontend Engineer at Zed, building the web experience around a code editor obsessed with speed and AI collaboration. As one of the few TypeScript developers in a Rust-first team, he bridges the gap between web and systems engineering – and knows how to keep three AI agents running live on stage without missing a beat.
Explore Gaauwe's work:
🔗  https://gaauwe.nl/
💻 Connect with Gaauwe:
🔗 https://github.com/gaauwe
Learn more about Zed:
🔗 https://zed.dev/

📝 Talk: Speed, Quality, and AI: You Can't Have It All (Or Can You?) | Gaauwe Rombouts

This talk was recorded at React Summit Amsterdam 2026:

01:47 - Building 3 projects simultaneously with audience's own stack

snippet.title: 3 Places Firebase Breaks Your Next.js UX (Auth, Firestore, Remote Config) | Rosário Fernandes
snippet.description:
🧩 Most Firebase flicker bugs aren't bugs. They're architectural mismatches between client SDKs and React Server Components. Here's how to fix all three – with FirebaseServerApp, onSnapshotResume, and Remote Config Server SDK.

What's inside:
⚡ The auth flicker: why your server renders a Sign In button even for logged-in users — and the fix
⚡ The Firestore flicker: offline cache + real-time updates = 2 extra renders. How to collapse them into zero
⚡ The Remote Config flicker: why feature flags flash on load and how server-side fetch eliminates it

🎙 Rosário P. Fernandes is a Developer Relations Engineer on the Firebase team at Google, helping developers build with Firebase and Generative AI across mobile and web. A passionate community builder, he's a regular presence at Android, Firebase, and Flutter developer events worldwide.
Connect with Rosário:
👉 https://twitter.com/thatfiredev
👉 https://linkedin.com/in/rosariopfernandes
👉 https://github.com/thatfiredev
👉 https://bsky.app/profile/thatfire.dev
Firebase:
👉 https://firebase.google.com/

📝 Talk: Taming the Flicker: Firebase Patterns for React Server Components | Rosario Fernandes

This talk was recorded at React Summit Amsterdam 2026: 
🔗 https://reactsummit.com/?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=RosarioFernandes

📌 Follow the link to watch the full version of all the conference talks, QnA’s with speakers and hands-on workshop recordings:  
🔗 https://gitnation.com/events/react-summit-2026?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=RosarioFernandes

🕐 Timestamps:
00:00 - "I'm Actually an Android Dev" — Rosário Takes the Stage
00:35 - Firebase Is a Client SDK. React Runs on the Server. Here's the Problem
01:18 - The Auth Flicker: Why Your App Shows a Login Button to Logged-In Users
01:57 - FirebaseServerApp: Fix Auth State Before the Page Even Loads
02:32 - Firestore Makes It Worse — Offline Cache + Real-Time = 3 Flickers Total
03:35 - toJSON() + onSnapshotResume: How to Serialize Your Way Out of the Flicker
04:39 - Remote Config on the Server: Feature Flags That Work Before JS Loads
05:16 - What's Next for Firebase on the Web (App Hosting, SQL Connect, Crashlytics)

snippet.title: One Rendering Layer for React, Angular & Vue. No Duplication - AG Grid Case Study | Stephen Cooper
snippet.description:
AG Grid supports React, Angular, Vue, and vanilla JS – from one codebase, without duplicating logic for each framework. Here's the architecture experiment behind it 🧪

What's inside:
⚡️ Why complex component logic can't live in the view layer – and where it should go instead
⚡️ How AG Grid stripped state, events, and tree structure out of framework components down to pure DOM primitives
⚡️ The abstraction layer that lets one component render natively in any framework – and why performance still holds

🎙 Stephen Cooper is Team Lead at AG Grid, 
where he's spent years deep in the codebase – optimizing grid performance, solving framework integrations, and profiling more render cycles than he'd care to admit. He brings practical, experience-based insights straight from production.
Connect with Stephen:
👉 https://twitter.com/scooperdev
👉 https://linkedin.com/in/scooper-dev
👉 https://github.com/StephenCooper
👉 https://scooper.dev/
Try AG Grid:
👉 https://www.ag-grid.com/

00:28 - When Your Component Logic Outgrows the Component
